Another method uses a graph-based search
and the basic idea behind this is that we create hubs.
And these hubs are kind of a small number of points
that are close to other points in the space.
And so we create some hubs and then we search from there.
So if we have a similar looking set of points in the space,
we find these hubs which are something like cluster centroids.
And then based on the cluster centroids,
we then rule down or we greatly reduce the number of points
that we need to be looking at.
And then we search through only those points
in a more kind of extensive manner.
And you can even turn this into a tree
where you have hubs and then you have kind of mini hubs
and then you have all the points.
So this allows you to do a kind of pre-based or graph-based search.

is that in order to form a good dense embedding,

transformer models are powerful.

it's also called late interaction in the Colbert paper.
But the way it works is you use contextualized representations
of all query and document tokens to compute a retrieval score.
And so you do offline indexing of every token
in the document.
And then based on this offline indexing of every token
in the document, you then have a query encoder,
and you do matching between each token in the query
and the highest scoring tokens in each document.
And the reason why this is good is still
allows you to encode all of the tokens in the document.
But each of these similarity searches
is still just a kind of maximum inner product search.
And because of this, this allows you to do each of these searches
efficiently and doesn't preclude you
from running it over an entire database.
The downside to this method may already be obvious.

we have a single vector for each document.
